Since our `PreparedRequest` now comes from [`self.session.prepare_request(self.request)`](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/models.py#L248-L260) instead of [`self.request.prepare()`](https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L426-L438) - see below - there is **a behavioural change in the default case (no `Session` explicitly provided)**.
    -  From the links above, you can see that this involves merging in `Session` default headers, cookies, params, auth, and hooks.  Of these, `params` and `auth` default to empty / none; I think the only interesting possibilities are around `cookies` and `headers`.
    -  If we consider the behavioural change acceptable, then we're good.  If on the other hand we want to ensure no behavioural change, we can change the definition of `DEFAULT_SESSION` to override cookies and headers to empty dictionaries.  Your call.
    -  Additionally (Python style question) should `DEFAULT_SESSION` be a method rather than an instance variable?  I worry that the lack of immutability means that somebody could accidentally change `DEFAULT_SESSION` and unintentionally affect other uses of it.

Correct. I changed the behavior to only merge ``__local_table_args__`` from all ``cls.__mro__``. The problem isn't so much with attempting to merge ``__table_args__``, but rather than in many cases this is useless, as an attempt to set ``__table_args__`` will result in overridding ``ModelBase.__table_args__`` so that no merge is even attempted. So I changed the paradigm to be that one never touches ``__table_args__`` but rather simply sets ``__local_table_args__`` wherever one wants (final class; intermediate class; mixin).

>  I don't have too strong an opinion on this. In general, I'd prefer to have fewer specialized functions, and instead just sanitize the input (eg by rounding to precision first). If that's not possible/sufficient here, for whatever reason, then we should keep it as is and leave match_events untouched until there's cause to change it.

The reason I'm rounding down is because I had a corner case in my unit tests where the distance between 2 events was exactly equal to the window, so when `<=` is used (as in `util.match_events`) the correct behavior would be to consider the events a match. However, without rounding down first the two events in my test were not being matched, and it turned out the reason was precision. So you could say this is an issue with `util.match_events`, since it uses `<=` but doesn't guarantee correct behavior for corner cases.

By default `django.contrib.messages` is included into `INSTALLED_APPS`. So unless a developer deleted the package from `INSTALLED_APPS` everything will work. If there is no `django.contrib.messages`  in `INSTALLED_APPS` an exception will be raised. But the default value of `LOGOUT_MESSAGE` is `None`, this means that by default we even don't need `django.contrib.messages`. And if a user will want to set `LOGOUT_MESSAGE` he will read the docs and see that it depends on `django.contrib.messages`. So the only scenario the exeption will be raised is when a user will specify `LOGOUT_MESSAGE` and delete `django.contrib.messages`  from `INSTALLED_APPS`.

@greut If we only enrich `~/.bashrc` then I guess people won't be able to do things like:

```consoly
$ docker run -i -t --rm invenio_web invenio3 --help
$ docker-compose run --rm web invenio3 --help
```

without knowing where `invenio3` got exactly installed.  They would have to either launch commands via bash, or specify full path.

Hence the `PATH` enrichment bit, where the static value is not so dirty considering that it matches Python docker defaults:

```console
$ docker run -i -t --rm python:2.7-slim /usr/bin/env | grep PATH
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
```

Anyway, to me, the bigger question is whether to use venv inside Docker *at all*.  OT1H, this is pretty superfluous, since we are already in a nicely isolated environment. OTOH, in this way we mimick exactly what non-Docker installation examples do. So there are some pros/cons. A natural alternative would be not to use venv inside Docker and simply install the invenio3 instance directly into `/usr/local/...` place;  then we wouldn't need to play with `PATH` and shell and friends.   We briefly considered pros/cons with @jirikuncar and decided to keep venv-inside-docker for the time being... but it would be probably more natural to kill it.

I pushed a commit that passes your unit tests (at least on my laptop).  I'm not 100% certain what was causing the problem, but I think it had something to do with `ChromaticConvolution.drawImage` creating copies of the things it was convolving and also creating entirely new objects as part if its strategy to fast track any separable components of the convolution.  I'm guessing these temporary objects did have their interpolation destroyed, but the originals did not.

So instead I tried destroying the interpolation when the transformation is first applied instead of waiting for drawImage to get called.  Seems to work!

-Josh

So looking at it again, I think my biggest issue is with direct exposure of the stack. Thinking through the interface from the perspective of someone new adding deployment support for a new resource, the biggest concern was the exposed stack interface and most of my suggestions (this third mode and the chaining suggestion) were related to how I would want to avoid it.

Specifically, my concerns with interacting with the stack were:
* If I pushed something onto the stack in an `execute()` call, what is the guarantee that the value pushed on will not affect a subsequent `execute()` call? And does that mean that I have to handle cleanup of what was pushed onto the stack? Right now this is protected by all of the `execute()` calls that we are doing starts with an `APICall()`, but there is no guarantee in the future. This concern was also furthered by the existence of a `Pop()` instruction that seemed to exist for the sole purpose of cleaning up values as no value is returned from the `Pop()` instruction.
* The stack interface opens the door for some poorly designed `execute()` workflows. Ideally, users of the interface should process the value for variable storage or resource recording immediately after the `Push()` or `APICall()`. But nothing is stopping them from making multiple  back to back `APICall()` or `Push()` and not immediately process the value for variable storage. Then at that point, it gets complicated because they need to keep track of the current state of all values in the stack. So like if they popped off a value, later down the road I would need to be aware that if they are storing is a variable or recording a resource value it will be of value that was underneath of what I had popped off.


I think a lot of these concerns would be satisfied if the stack was switched to more of a register, where:
* The register stores the value from the previous `APICall`. So if you want to store it or record it do so then as after the next `APICall` it will be a new value.
* On the call of every `execute()`, the register is cleared to start so there is no previous state.

That way it forces me to interact with it more in the way that is recommended and I do not have to worry about state carrying over.


Then as to the comment about the third mode of `RecordResourceValue`, could we just have different classes for these instructions? So to make sure the instruction class is not too complicated so we could break this out to a `RecordResourceFromValue`, `RecordResourceFromVariable`, and `RecordResourceFromStack` (or register or whatever comes out of this).

`get_token_embedder` is a function that returns a `Module`.  A `Module` in pytorch is the same as a `Layer` in keras; i.e., it's an object that takes tensors as input and returns tensors as output. The distinction between `Variable` and `Tensor` in pytorch is a bit awkward, and is supposed to be going away in pytorch 0.4.  We've been annotating types with `torch.Tensor` instead of `Variable`, partially looking ahead to the type change that's coming.  So, unless mypy complains, I'd switch everywhere that you have `Variable` annotations to `torch.Tensor` annotations (and if you can be more specific, like `torch.LongTensor` or `torch.FloatTensor`, even better).

`tabulator` could use it as a `source` with `format=native`. First version of code was just relaying on autodetection.

```python
tabulator.Stream('id,name\n1,english', format='csv)
```

So for our `resource.data`:
```
tabulator.Stream([['id', 'name'], [1, 'english']], format='native)
```

I find it a bit strange that `_tzparser` re-uses the lexer like this. I see three other possible ways of going here:

1. Split out `_timelexer` and `_tzlexer` into two different lexers with independent rules.
2. Do some sort of token splitting downstream based on the context.
3. Reverse  the commit that keeps the comma token together and dynamically re-join it as necessary.

None of them are particularly satisfying.

Another way to go with your approach is to come up with some more satisfying name for that flag - if there's some unifying theme for the cases where you'd want it to be true, then I'm more OK with adding the flag.

The thing is, 2 and 3 seem wrong because they violate separation of concerns, but we may need to do either 2 or 3 if we want to also solve #203 and other comma-related problems caused by this.

If we implemented this, I would definitely want the dots to be dismissed after we're done waiting for idle, since they're just noise.

It looks like we're only calling `wait_for_idle` to wait before creating the SQL context; we never actually call that method before cell executions.  We could add another call to this method before cell executions if you wanted to implement this.

I'm not sure if I like the idea all that much myself.  Usually dots that programs pop up suggest the program is busy doing some useful work, when the opposite is true here.  It might be confusing for users if they sporadically get 1 or 2 dots, and then get no more dots for another 10 minutes before their result is displayed. 

What is it that you would want to communicate with output text like this?  I think it might be better to identify those scenarios, recognize when they happen, and pop up with disposable text messages that capture what's happening for the user (e.g. "This query is taking longer than usual", etc.)

A few thoughts on this:

First, while it is a bit of a nitpick, I would ask that the positional argument be spelled `fn` to match `c.f.Future` and `asyncio`. It is possible to pass positional arguments using their keyword names, and many people do.

Second, I am having a debate with myself over the thread here. Part of me thinks that this method should _return_ the `Thread` object (so that the programmer would have the option of calling `thread.join()` later). On the other hand, I do not particularly want to be tied to this exact implementation; we might want to change this to use gevent or something. Since `c.f.Future` and `asyncio` do not expect this to return, probably we should leave it.

Finally, it is worth noting that the `threading` module is relatively poorly implemented in Python and is generally disfavored (with [`multiprocess`][1] being preferred). If this is something you can change quickly, it would probably be preferable. That said, I also think it is not the end of the world if that is time-consuming, especially since @bjwatson has already fingered this spot for a potential improvement as we discover how it is actually used.

  [1]: https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html#module-multiprocessing

OKAY, I figured out the problem with `StopIteration` and `async_generator`!

Here it is: when a `StopIteration` gets thrown into an `async_generator`, it actually ends up inside the magic `await yield_()` function. But Python doesn't know that it's supposed to be a magic function that acts like `yield`; it just thinks its some random async function. And then it tries to raise `StopIteration`, and Python's like uh, no, haven't you heard of PEP 479? Async functions can't raise `StopIteration`. So it immediately wraps it into a `RuntimeError`. Which ... is a little weird and annoying, but there's nothing we can do about it, and it doesn't stop us from unwrapped it later. Except... the way this particular test is written, where it wraps everything in a `RuntimeError`, means that we end up wrapping our `StopIteration` in a `RuntimeError` *twice*. We get `RuntimeError` → `RuntimeError` → `StopIteration`.

So there is something of a bug here, but it's intrinsic to how `async_generator` works, and we can't fix that part.

But! We can still test the main thing we care about, which is that a `StopIteration` is able to pass through a context manager like
```python
@acontextmanager
@async_generator
async def simpler_context_manager():
    await yield_()
```

And this should work, though slightly differently than if it were a native generator. With a native generator, the `yield` would raise `StopIteration`, which would then get wrapped in a `RuntimeError` when it tried to leave the function. With `async_generator`, the `await yield_()` directly wraps the `StopIteration` into a `RuntimeError`, and then that propagates through the function and out. But either way we can catch it and unwrap it in `@acontextmanager`.
